topic: The Changing Landscapes of the UK
subject: geography

items.q: Is the UK's pattern of uplands and lowlands random?
items.correct: No — older, harder igneous and metamorphic rocks dominate the north and west uplands, while younger, softer sedimentary rocks underlie the south-eastern lowlands; geology controls relief.
items.misconception: Where hills and plains occur in the UK has no underlying explanation.
items.random_wrong: Uplands are piles of soil left by medieval builders.
items.why: Rock type and structure explain the upland-lowland contrast that anchors this topic.

items.q: Are UK landscapes shaped only by processes operating today?
items.correct: No — glaciation during past ice ages carved much of the upland scenery, and past tectonic and climatic conditions set the framework that rivers and waves now modify.
items.misconception: Only today's rivers, waves and weather have shaped what we see.
items.random_wrong: UK landscapes were designed in Victorian times.
items.why: Recognising relict glacial features distinguishes inherited landforms from active processes.

items.q: Do humans play only a minor role in changing UK landscapes?
items.correct: No — farming, forestry, quarrying, settlement and coastal management have transformed most UK landscapes, altering rates of erosion, deposition and drainage.
items.misconception: UK scenery is almost entirely natural and untouched.
items.random_wrong: Humans are legally barred from altering landscapes.
items.why: Human activity is an explicit strand of this topic that students tend to underplay.

items.q: Which line divides upland and lowland Britain?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Tees-Exe Line. “Watford Line” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Where are softer sedimentary rocks found in UK?
items.why: It's Southeast England. “Scottish Highlands” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Approximately how many km of coastline does the UK have?
items.why: The real figure is 17820 km. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
